content.assessment: Reciprocal contracts could restore some local bargaining power over closure decisions, potentially benefiting villages currently powerless under full centralization (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02). But this lens's evidence indicates that villages retaining schools do not uniformly have better socioeconomic conditions (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06 - weak), so contract-based retention decisions risk entrenching disparities based on local negotiating capacity rather than need. Municipalities with weaker fiscal capacity - likely correlating with disadvantaged and Roma populations concentrated in North-East Hungary (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05) - may be least able to negotiate favorable reciprocal terms, potentially widening rather than narrowing distributional gaps.
opportunities: Could be designed with equity-weighted co-financing rules that give greater fiscal support to disadvantaged municipalities, correcting for their negotiating disadvantage
Contract renewal cycles could track distributional and segregation outcomes over time
strengths: Reintroduces local voice into decisions currently made entirely by central authorities, potentially allowing communities to advocate for equity-relevant local conditions
Time-bound structure could allow periodic equity reassessment rather than permanent one-way closure decisions
threats: Risk that a state-initiated contractual instrument, without genuine municipal capacity, becomes asymmetric and mainly benefits municipalities that already have relatively more resources, replicating a two-tier pattern similar to the church-versus-public school divide (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
Absence of standing fiscal capacity among Hungarian municipalities compared to French communes could mean the most disadvantaged rural areas gain the least from this mechanism
weaknesses: Weak evidence (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06) that school presence itself tracks favorable socioeconomic conditions undermines assumption that contract-based retention targets genuinely viable/deserving villages

Fiscal capacity to negotiate and co-finance is likely lowest precisely in the most disadvantaged rural municipalities, especially in already-disadvantaged North-East Hungary (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05)
